episode_title: Gina Chua: Where Journalism's Value Lives When AI Tells the Story

codes.description: The journalistic process (asking questions, collecting facts, adding context, analysis, narration, distribution, engagement) is being unbundled by AI, with narration increasingly automated and value shifting to the front-end work of questioning and audience understanding.
excerpts: we ask questions, we collect facts, we add context, we do analysis on it, and we create a narrative. We distribute and in an idea we engage afterwards.
we're in a world where a lot of the narration work is going to be done by machines, or can be done by machines. And if that's the case, then where's our value on this value chain?
I suspect that a lot of the value comes from that initial work of what are the questions
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Provocative reframing of journalism's mission away from preserving jobs or institutional forms and toward ensuring public-interest information reaches people.
excerpts: We're not in the business of saving journalists. We're not necessarily even in the business of saving journalism. We're in the business of saving information in the public interest
If we were all doctors, you wouldn't be saying, Well what about the doctors? We're not here to save doctors. We're not even here to save medicine, we're here to save public health
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: The prospect of AI creating multiple versions of the same story tailored to individual readers' knowledge, interests, and preferred formats, moving from one-to-many to one-to-one journalism.
excerpts: the model we have is writing a single story and trying to get it to as many people as possible... I can't possibly write the twenty five versions of stories that would reach twenty-five different people. But a machine can let us do that.

when somebody a story says the court case, you know, so and so was accused of such and such. And you look at it and you say, well I know that because I read the story yesterday. So why do you need to tell me that again
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: AI as a means to address systemic gaps in journalism's coverage of marginalized communities, exemplified by the Homicide Watch DC case where a 1.5-person team covered every murder while the Washington Post covered only a newsworthy subset.
excerpts: How many of those does the Washington Post cover? Well, okay. It covers a subset of 260, right? It does not cover it. Which ones does it cover? And you can kind of guess which ones they cover
a whole bunch of families and lives are not considered important by our industry
Homicide Watch and they covered every murder. And that's the sorts of things that I think we should be thinking of and the sorts of things that that generative AI can allow us to do
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Non-engineers (journalists/editors) using conversational AI tools like Claude to build functional software tools through natural language rather than traditional programming.
excerpts: two of us are basically vibe coding our way through everything with Claude, which by the way I have a very deep and personal relationship with Claude now
what you really want to do is you want to take the the moments of epiphany that you have... And the thing with with vibe coding is you can do that
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Chua's pedagogical framing of LLMs as a knowledgeable but unreliable English major named Dylan who needs reference materials (RAG) to be trustworthy.
excerpts: an LLM is a smart, slightly autistic, but very friendly English major. I call him Dylan. He has a beret and a cigarette holder. He slept through every class, but he's very good at English

If you ask Dylan a question, you know, when did Benjamin Franklin die, he'll make up the answer because he slept through History class. Give him a book and you say read it and tell me when Benjamin Franklin die. And he will tell you
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation identified as the foundational architectural pattern for building trustworthy journalism AI tools, enabling domain-specific accuracy by grounding LLMs in curated documents.
excerpts: Once I came to that conclusion, everything I did was a rag
the whole trick is to give Dylan books, which is rags
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: LLM models are unreliable over time; tools built on them break without warning when underlying training changes, creating a fundamental engineering challenge.
excerpts: the models aren't stable. They break, you build something, three weeks later it works fine, three weeks later it that stops working. You're not sure why, because they've changed something, you know, in the underlying training
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: The dominant obstacle to AI adoption in newsrooms is not the technology itself but integrating tools into existing editorial workflows and habits.
excerpts: the problem has been up until now is, you know, it's workflow. It's always workflow. It's always been workflow.
it worked, but it was not as smooth and as functional as it should have been
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Getting journalists to adopt new tools is fundamentally a social engineering problem requiring phased strategies from enthusiasts to reluctant middle to forced adoption.
excerpts: you wind up doing a sort of a slow strategy, right? Where you where you bring on the enthusiasts, you make it easy enough for the for the vast middle to just play... And then when you get to the final group, I think you basically change the tools so they have no choice
we don't spend enough time, I think, thinking about sort of design thinking and and workflow and sort of the social engineering needed to help people get along
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Legacy content management systems create deep institutional inertia; Reuters spent 30 years trying to replace its CMS, and new systems succeed only when they mimic old interfaces.
excerpts: Reuters very famously spent like 30 years trying to change its CMS. Experiment after experiment, and they all failed millions of dollars
the only reason we could get links adopted by people is because links look exactly like system seventy seven, including all of the horrible architecture
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Strategic imperative to begin with what serves the audience rather than what preserves existing business models, then work backward to sustainability.
excerpts: I think we have to start with the question What will generative AI do to the audience? How will it change how people come to information
we go to war with the audience we have, not the audience we would like to have
start by figuring out what is good for people and then figure out how we can play a role in bringing it to them and then hopefully pay the bills
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Call for radical reinvention of journalism rather than incremental adaptation, treating the current moment as an opportunity to question every assumption.
excerpts: We are now in a place, in a position, in a time, you call it an opportunity or you can call it a rolling disaster, whichever you want to call it. But the world is going to change... If we're going to reinvent journalism, let's burn it to the studs and think about what we should do and can do and who we can truly serve
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Acknowledgment that the majority of daily news output is formulaic enough to be machine-generated, distinguishing between commodity news and high-value journalism.
excerpts: the vast majority of the newspaper is four hundred word stories that, you know, essentially are functionally as close to written by a machine as you could imagine. And you know, there's no reason why in fact the machine can't do some of that
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Legal uncertainty around whether AI-generated content is copyrightable, threatening journalism's business model which relies on copyrighting the form of words.
excerpts: it is at this point at least, uh not settled law, but legal understanding that a completely AI created artifact is not copyrightable. And what does that mean if you can't copyright the form of words because right now you can't copyright facts
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Meta's move toward AI-generated personalized ads signals a parallel future for personalized news content, using the same underlying technology.
excerpts: The Wall Street Journal had this story yesterday about how Meta was working on getting the the capability to build customized advertising on the fly... So why can't we do that with stories? And why shouldn't we do that with stories?
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Individual voices, authentic relationships, and distinctive editorial perspectives identified as journalism's most defensible assets against AI disruption.
excerpts: there is real value in voices and real value and sort of authenticity... individuals, influencers, podcasters, substackers, I think they have a path forward because people want their want their voice, they want their view
Liz has a great take on the world. And so you're not coming to her necessarily for for just the information, you're coming for for her view of how the world is
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: MISO tool enabling journalists to search, translate, and summarize news across multiple languages simultaneously, expanding source diversity beyond linguistic barriers.
excerpts: you write a search term in, it would translate it into multiple languages, it would go off and it would search in those languages... it would translate those stories, it would summarize those stories, and it would put them out
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Aspiration to build tools that can separate verifiable facts from editorial framing, assumptions, and analysis within news stories, making journalistic choices transparent.
excerpts: I want to build a tool that you can put any story in and it will extract the verifiable facts and it will extract the assumptions and analysis and then it will tell you the framing
in a move likely to cost him the election, comma congressman, John Smith was caught stealing candy from a baby... There's two things in that story
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Arguing that AI tool accuracy should be evaluated against the realistic baseline (often no professional resource at all) rather than against a gold standard few can access.
excerpts: what is the error rate on security advice when you have no security advice? The point is what are you comparing against?

is it better to have a thousand dollar a day security advisor walking next to you... Or is it better to have, you know, some information that's come to you on a bot beforehand? I think I'd rather have the thousand dollar a day person, but I don't have the thousand dollar a day person
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: The Jess safety bot as an example of AI democratizing access to expert knowledge previously available only at large, well-resourced organizations like Reuters.
excerpts: there's lots of news organizations that can't afford security advisors, don't have, as we had at Reuters, nine people full-time doing this. So why not try to bring that information to the world?
because Dylan is multilingual We can do it in a zillion languages... you can give security advice in Hindi or or Tagalog or Nepali or you know French or Portuguese
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Voice cloning technology used to restore communication capability for a friend with ALS, illustrating AI's potential for human dignity beyond journalism.
excerpts: I have a very good friend who has ALS... we could do was find clips of his old voice and build clone it and now build him a little handheld app where he can speak in his own voice
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Systematic experimental approach to understanding what LLMs do well and poorly, compared to evaluating a new employee's strengths and weaknesses.
excerpts: mostly I'm just trying to test the edges of what LLMs do well and what they do badly. It's like testing out a new employee. Right. You want to know like they're good at this... let's figure out where the skill sets are
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: The industry's biggest constraint is not technology or resources but a failure of imagination about what AI could enable.
excerpts: the really the biggest deficit we have as an industry is imagination. We're not thinking big enough.
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Rapidly shrinking estimates for when agentic AI will be ready for newsroom deployment, reflecting accelerating technological change.
excerpts: if again if you asked me a year ago, I'd have said five years. Now you ask me, I'm thinking, you know, a year and a half, two years, and if you ask me next week, I'll probably say six months
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Senior leaders learning from younger colleagues who have grown up with different technological assumptions and see possibilities invisible to veterans.
excerpts: I'm old and I'm learning a ton from everybody in Semaphore who's who's much younger than me

Ben Smith is is, you know, I don't know, like ten or fifteen years younger than me. And I'm learning a zillion things from him every day because he's grown up in a time where he sees things differently
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: The only guaranteed losing strategy is assuming the news industry will remain unchanged; change is inevitable even if its direction is unknowable.
excerpts: do not bet on it being the same, because that is a losing bet
If you'd asked me this question two years ago, I'm not sure this would be my answer. And if you ask me next week, I might change my mind again
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Candid acknowledgment that journalism has systematically failed marginalized communities on civil rights, AIDS, LGBT coverage, and women's suffrage, challenging the profession's self-image.
excerpts: we also tend to think of this either we either valorize what we do as a gold standard... But we also have not always served our communities well. We have underserved Tons of communities. Pick your topic. Civil rights, AIDS, LGBT coverage. Women's suffrage.
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Using RAG-based classifiers with nuance scales for content moderation, hate crime detection, and misinformation identification, moving beyond binary judgments.
excerpts: I built the uh the hate crime classifier, it struck me like this is a perfect tool for Content moderation... Here are my written down rules about, you know, what content should or shouldn't be. There's a lot of gray area in there.
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Future vision of AI-mediated news consumption as a personalized valet that manages information flow based on individual preferences, schedules, and intensity of interest.
excerpts: I think that it becomes much more like a valet. And you can figure out what's the the best way you want... we may decide we want to go on news diets and so like don't disturb me about news from, you know, nine o'clock to twelve o'clock unless the world has blown up
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

codes.description: Contrast between a small startup (Semafor) where everyone is young and eager to experiment versus large legacy newsrooms with entrenched resistance and decades of technical debt.
excerpts: we don't have that problem at Sever 4 because everybody is really, really young. I'm the oldest person in the newsroom by like fifteen years. And so like everybody's game to try everything

At Reuters there was always going to be some group of people who are really interested in testing things, a big mass of people who are like maybe, and then a group of people that really don't want to do it
codes.speaker: Gina Chua

episode_summary: Gina Chua presents a radical reconceptualization of journalism's value chain in the age of AI, arguing that narration -- long considered the core of the craft -- will increasingly be automated, shifting value to the front-end work of asking questions, uncovering facts, and deeply understanding audiences. She challenges the profession's self-image by pointing to historical failures in serving marginalized communities and positions AI personalization as a potential corrective, not just a business innovation. Her practical work at Semafor (vibe coding with Claude, building RAG-based tools like MISO and safety bot Jess) demonstrates how small teams can prototype consequential tools, while her broader framework -- comparing AI error rates against realistic baselines rather than gold standards -- offers a pragmatic philosophical stance. The episode reveals a fundamental tension between Chua's enthusiasm for burning journalism 'to the studs' and the deep institutional inertia she experienced across decades at Reuters, where even CMS changes took thirty years.
surprises: Chua explicitly says journalism has not served communities well on civil rights, AIDS, and LGBT issues, using this as justification for AI-driven reinvention rather than preservation.
She frames the safety bot debate not around AI accuracy but around the baseline of zero professional safety support most journalists actually have.
A senior editor spending hours conversationally coding with Claude to build production tools, blurring the line between editorial leadership and software development.
The voice cloning project for a friend with ALS, illustrating deeply personal motivations for engaging with AI beyond professional applications.
tensions: Between burning journalism 'to the studs' and the thirty-year CMS replacement struggle at Reuters -- radical vision versus institutional reality.
Between personalized AI-generated news and the loss of editorial voice, byline attribution, and copyright protection.
Between democratizing safety knowledge through imperfect AI (Jess) and the life-and-death stakes raised by the NYT audience member.
Between serving the audience we have (who resist change) and building for the audience we need.
Between Chua's provocative 'not saving journalists' stance and the real human cost of industry layoffs she acknowledges.
